summary: Gruber examines the sizing problems customers are experiencing with Apple's new Solo Loop and Braided Solo Loop bands for Apple Watch. Because the bands aren't adjustable and come in nine precise sizes, many buyers are getting poor fits using Apple's printable measuring tool. Worse, since the watch and band are sold as a single SKU, customers must return the entire Apple Watch to exchange a band, then wait weeks for replacements due to supply constraints. Gruber argues this is Apple's first real bite from COVID-era restrictions, since both accurate sizing and easy exchanges depend on in-person retail experiences. He suspects most ill-fitting loops are too loose because people are using the measuring tool incorrectly, and notes that like buying shoes, there's simply no substitute for trying them on.
key_points: Apple's Solo Loop bands require precise wrist sizing but the printable measuring tool is proving inaccurate for many customers
Returning an ill-fitting band requires returning the entire Apple Watch since they're sold as a bundled SKU, creating weeks-long waits for replacements
This is arguably Apple's first product launch genuinely hurt by COVID restrictions on in-person retail
Most sizing errors seem to result in bands that are too loose, suggesting people are measuring incorrectly rather than the tool itself being flawed
Non-adjustable precisely-sized watch bands are unprecedented in the watch industry for good logistical reasons
money_quotes: This sucks, but you can see how it happened. I think this is the first situation where Apple has been not just hindered, but outright bitten by COVID-era restrictions.
There's a reason why no watch bands from any watch brand I'm aware of are sized as precisely as Apple's new Solo Loops. It's a huge logistical problem compared to adjustable watch bands.
The whole thing is premised on people knowing their correct size — which is a function both of their actual wrist size and their preference for how loose or tight they prefer bands to feel.
Again, Apple PR's size guessers are freakishly good.
I think you're supposed to use the tool to precisely measure the circumference of your wrist, not to simulate the circumference of what you think would be a comfortable watch band.
So again, like buying shoes, there's no substitute for trying them on in person. Which, alas, is not an option for a lot of us at the moment.
key_insight: Apple's precisely-sized Solo Loop bands represent an unprecedented logistical challenge in watchmaking that fundamentally depends on in-person fitting — a dependency painfully exposed by COVID-era retail restrictions.
